text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Hey everybody, I wanted to take a quick minute before we get going to let you know about the new YouTube channel that I recently started.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: On this channel, I teach software developers and mobile developers and specifically how to become independent consultants.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you've ever wanted to become an independent consultant but weren't sure how, then this is the channel for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'll talk about the billing rates that you need to set, how to find clients, how to find an evergreen source of clients,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: how to stay on new technology trends, how to promote yourself, how to market yourself, how to get insurance, you name it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If it's around working for yourself and independent consulting and software, I'll be talking about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Ultimately, what being an independent consultant will do for you is provide you with more financial freedom and the autonomy to work for yourself and set your own hours.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: This is something that appeals to a lot of people.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And if it appeals to you, then please check out my YouTube channel.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And you can see that at donfelker.com slash YouTube, or just check out the link in the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Again, that's donfelker.com slash YouTube.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That'll take you directly to the YouTube channel.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Thanks for checking it out.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Let's get to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Everybody, welcome back to the show this week.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We're missing Kaushik again.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: He'll be back soon enough.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But in the meantime, there's a topic that I've been wanting to talk about for quite a while that comes up in every single company that I worked for since, you know, working for any company in general.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And that is DevOps.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And a lot of times DevOps is delegated to a particular system administrator or someone who just happens to know more than another person, meaning the developer just happens to be good at it and it's kind of handed off to them.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I thought this would be a great opportunity to bring on a friend of mine who has been my basically DevOps person that I use for all of my own personal projects, as well as many other clients that I've had.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I've referred him over to.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So without further ado, I'd like to welcome Will Button to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Welcome to the show, Will.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Hey, thanks man.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Thanks for having me on.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, definitely.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So one of the reasons why I wanted to bring you on is because anytime I run into a situation that requires any type of DevOps, it could be, hey, I have something in the back end.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I don't know how to handle this or how do I monitor this situation or whatever that's not developer related.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I usually reach out to you and you've been my go to person for a number of years in that regard.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And we've actually worked together at a couple of clients.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I think you have the most experience out of anybody I know of in regards to DevOps.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I wanted to bring you on to talk about it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But before we get into kind of like some nitty gritty, I was wondering if you might be able to, in your own words, explain what DevOps is.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: yeah so to me devops i think the whole concept of devops has been a failed marketing opportunity because a lot of people have this conception that devops specifically with the title of devops engineer is something that you hire someone for when really it goes much further beyond that devops itself
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: is not really who does what, but it's how we go about the business of getting code from a developer's workstation into production.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it just, it kind of defines the way that we want that to happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it can be different for every company.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You know, everyone, every company may have a different Git workflow, definitely has a different infrastructure.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But what are the key points that we hit along that path
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: to make sure that you know we're hitting hitting everything consistently and when we deploy it's being done the same way it's being done reliably and there's monitoring and logging around that so that when things don't go right everyone knows about it
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it's just kind of automating both the technology that makes that happen and automating the workflow so that it integrates into the developer's daily routine and happens just through automation rather than someone having to remember, oh, I gotta go do this thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's a good point.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I've seen this stuff happen in action at one of the previous clients we both worked at.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So the majority of folks that listen to this show are probably mobile devs, though we do have a number of web devs as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I know that you've worked at various startups that have had mobile applications and stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Now, in the context of mobile development, I guess we could kind of lump in APIs there too.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What does DevOps mean in the context of mobile?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Or what are some of the things that people have to think about in regards to DevOps from a mobile perspective?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: The one that jumps out to me immediately is a consistent deployment process, you know, so that as a mobile developer, whenever new features are built on the back end, that they go out to a staging environment where you can start developing your code against those and then a high level of confidence that whatever you wrote with against staging is going to operate the same out in production.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So like if I have, maybe I make an API change and, or I'm a backend developer, I make an API change and I push it up to a new branch that somehow gets into a staging environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Does it get its own staging environment or does it just kind of go into a common one?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It really depends on the team and the complexity of the environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's some places I've worked that have been smaller applications.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It just goes into a staging environment that's shared by everyone.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Other places as teams have grown and they've broken from like a monolith type environment into more of a microservices type environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: then it will be specific to that microservice.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then I've also built it out so that it can be specific to a feature.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So when a developer creates a new feature branch for an API and they push that code up to the repo, then it launches a new version of the API from that feature branch.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you can test out that specific branch of code before it's merged anywhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So, okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I guess that would mean it has its own URL somewhere just for that particular branch.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So what we do there in that case is it's built into the CI CD workflow and just triggers off of GitHub.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So whenever a new merge request or pull request is opened up on a specific branch, that triggers the GitHub that causes a CI CD server to go out and build an environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And when it builds that, it builds the Docker images that are necessary, launches the containers, sets up the load balancers.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then finally creates a DNS name where the DNS name, the first part of the DNS name is just the name of the feature branch from GitHub.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay, so maybe if you just kind of replace anything that had like slashes with like hyphens or something like that to give it a URL name?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you brought up a couple of interesting technologies there, namely Docker, and this seems like it's very popular in the DevOps world.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Why is Docker so popular in DevOps?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is it because it enables these types of environments or what's the allure for it?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I think there's a couple of reasons that everyone gets excited about Docker.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: One of the primary reasons
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: is it helps break that works on my machine syndrome.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You know, if you've got specific packages or software configuration on your local workstation, your code may work fine.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But then when you put that out on a staging or production server, if you're missing that configuration, it fails.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But now you're already in production, so you've got an outage potentially and you have to roll back.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So with Docker, what that allows you to do is use the same Docker image or the same base image on your workstation that you're going to use out in production.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you're mimicking the production environment very, very closely when you're doing your local development and can identify any changes or updates that are needed while you're still in development mode rather than once you've gone to production mode.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So it seems like it would be pretty easy for me to, I mean, if I know how to work with Docker, which I do, but for folks that don't, but if they were able to learn about it, they could then basically clone a repository, tell Docker to fire up, which would probably be through Docker Compose, I guess.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then at that point, it would have almost like a mimicked version of production running that they could interact with, correct?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's one of the best metrics.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you are trying to actively integrate DevOps into your engineering team, I think that's one of the best metrics to determine success is think about...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: onboarding a new developer, what's the length of time measured in hours from handing that developer their laptop to their first commit to the repo?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And Docker really enables you to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Prior to Docker, it may be something like, oh, the developer has to download this, they've got to install this, they've got to install MySQL, and they've got to install Node.js, and they've got to get their...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: mobile development environment set up, you know, whether that's Android Studio or Flutter or something like that and build all these different pieces and magically get them sealed together inside of their laptop.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Whereas if you go with a more DevOps oriented approach, you know, you could get it down to where it's as simple as here's your laptop, install Docker, check out this repo and type make dev up and it just brings everything up for them.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay, you said make.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What is make?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I know a lot of folks are familiar with things like Gradle and they're familiar with NPM and various things like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But I mean, I hear make, I think of like C++, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, that's exactly where it's from.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's the old C++ compiler, which seems out of context to use here, but it's really powerful and works on so many different systems.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it just gives you a way to simplify multiple complex commands so that everyone can use the same command.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if I type make and you said dev up, is that something that's built into to make or what is that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Now, whenever you type make, it looks in whatever directory you're currently in and looks for what's called a make file.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And inside the make file is where you'll define your arguments for the make command.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So in that example, one of the things I commonly do is create a command called dev op.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then that dev op command runs the Docker Compose command.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And Docker Compose looks through the Docker Compose file to find out what all containers and networks it needs to bring up.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then each of those containers has different specifications.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Like one, if it's a node API, it'll run the npm start command.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or if it's a Postgres database, it'll launch Postgres.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so just using make abstracts all of that away down to a single command.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So I know what a lot of people are probably thinking right now, like, all right, well, why not just use like some Ruby script or why not use a JavaScript file?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Like what, you know, what is the, why should I use make?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is it because it's, is it installed by default or what's the lure there?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's exactly why it's already there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: No dependencies required.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you do NPM scripts, you've got to have
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: node installed, or if you have, you know, Ruby scripts, you've got to have Ruby installed and it makes just agnostic and exists everywhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Oh, so you could, okay, so that makes sense.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And you could just, do you check that make file?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I guess it's almost like a, I think of it like a, almost like an alias or almost like a build file in itself, or it's, do you check that into the source control too?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, absolutely.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So let me walk through the scenario here.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'm a new developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I show up first day on the job.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I'm an Android developer.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That's all cake to me.

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And from there, there's just a make file in there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: D-E-V up.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's all one word, make dev up and then hit enter and everything should just fire up is what it should be, huh?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yep, exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Seems kind of magical almost.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: it is once you get it working well yeah yeah once you get it working it is and it's not really um any great feat of technology to get it working it's just taking a step back and saying how can i make this easier and you know the technology i use to make it work is is just using make which has been around since what the 80s i think
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, it's been around a long time.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So let's say I got this up and running and now I'm done for the day and I need to shut my laptop down or whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I need to reboot some updates.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: There is another command called make dev down.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And what does that one do?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That one tears down all of the dependency environments.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's all built into the command.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So depending on what dependencies you have, it will take care of all of those for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It'll do a Docker Compose shutdown.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If there's Docker networks and Docker volumes that need to be cleaned up, that'll be built into it as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: interesting okay so that's an easy way to set up your local dev environment for you know web stuff like that have you seen anybody do this a similar type of thing for mobile or no not really so much for mobile because it's um you know you're heavily dependent on the emulators or having a local device plugged in and doing the yeah you know the deployment over usb
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Interesting.

text: Um, back to the, I'm really interested in the whole developing, um, making a change to the API, pushing it out to a branch and that branch becomes a, a new DNS entry and all that's like automated, you said.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so I imagine there's a number of tools that are involved in this process.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What are some of the common tools that you use to automate all of these things?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Like if I wanted to get that type of scenario up and running,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, so it depends a little bit on where your infrastructure is hosted, whether you're using AWS or GCP or you have your own data center.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: For AWS, I rely on cloud formation heavily for that because it knows.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: about all of the dependencies.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if you have Docker containers and load balancers and DNS entries, AWS is aware of the relationships between those.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so just defining it in the CloudFormation template, it'll look through there and say, oh, well, I can't do this until I do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So it takes care of a lot of that for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Outside of that, you can use tools like Ansible or Terraform to do the same thing, which gets you into a more cloud agnostic situation where you can use that across different cloud providers.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay, so even if I had, maybe I'm in a big corporation and we don't use cloud providers and I have my own infrastructure internally, I could use something like Ansible or Terraform internally to mimic that same type of situation then.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, for sure.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And there's a lot of overlap between the features in Terraform and Ansible.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you could just look at each one and see which one
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: which one you think you'll have better luck with, because it's really a trial and error type thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's one of the most frustrating things about DevOps for a lot of people just getting started into it is the trial and error stage is very, very time consuming.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You've got to build Docker containers.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you get through all of that only to find out that you had a syntax error in your YAML file or something.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So 10 minutes later, it blows up.

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you don't have a lot of senior in-house tech resources early on in your startup, totally recommend using something like Heroku just because of the ease of getting up and running is so much easier.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it allows you to focus on delivering that product and testing your idea.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then once that idea is successful, you can come back and figure out, okay, what are we going to do to run this long term?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that's where I ended up going in and helping those guys out, moving them from Heroku to AWS, cut their infrastructure operating bill by about a third, if I remember correctly.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Wow.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that's, I mean, if you're going from five figures, let's just say you're $15,000 a month, you cut it to a third of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I mean, that's, you know, 5,000, that's, you just save them $10,000 a month by switching from Heroku to AWS.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But you bring up a very good point.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And that's one that I make all the time is like, you don't, you know, premature optimization will kill you initially.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If you try to do all this starting out when you're building your product and you don't even know if it's going to generate revenue or profit, like you're just fighting a losing battle.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Oh, absolutely.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's this tendency in the tech world to leverage every latest framework and technology and best practice right off the gate for your startup.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's the wrong approach.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The first step should be validating, are people actually willing to give me money for this?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, and then once you get to that point, like you said, once you get to that point, if your bill is getting that high at Heroku or similar service, like you said, they're charging a surcharge on top of putting a pretty wrapper around a very difficult thing to manage, which is AWS, because that's what Heroku runs on.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So they're making it easy to use AWS, basically, and you're given that little dial.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And so if I need more capacity, I just drag the dial and they charge my credit card more money.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It's about as easy as it gets.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I need more RAM.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I need more resources.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Like, okay, cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Just pay more and they'll make it happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So that's the benefit there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And people have to think about that behind the scenes.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Like, okay, how much would this cost me to build behind the scenes in an hourly manage?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And it's not just build.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: That means maintain and also all the bugs you're going to introduce, which is going to be a tremendous amount.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then versus clicking and dragging a button.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, it might be three times the cost, but you're going to save 10 X a time probably.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, and for some companies, it may never make sense to try and go from Heroku to something like AWS, just depending on their staffing, their resources, and the technical requirements of their application.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, I still have a couple of friends who run some SaaS applications.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: They generate hundreds of thousands of dollars a month in revenue, have staff on hand.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I mean, they generate millions and millions of dollars of revenue a year, and they still run a Heroku.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And for the simple reason is we don't want to manage everything else.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So they delegate it off to another service and pay the premium for it.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And as long as it works in your profit margin, there's no reason to change.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I think this brings up a good question here is, if you were to give folks, the developers that are listening to the show, a recommendation of,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I like to use the 80-20 rule a lot.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So if you were to give them 20% of things or tools or whatever that they could implement to give them 80% of the benefits of DevOps, what would it be?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I mean, it could be things like Heroku or just learning technologies or what would be your take on that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: think it's a little bit context dependent you know i always like to use uh tim ferris's rule of what would it look like if this were easy yeah and then you know just think about the different things that are causing you stress and frustration and say well what if that didn't exist and start there typically the common places i see that
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: are in automating the development workflow, which is kind of a broad umbrella, but it means when you leave the Scrum and you've got your ticket that you create a new feature branch off of master, you do your work, you commit that branch, you open a pull request,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That pull request gets reviewed.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then upon merging, that kicks off a CICD pipeline that deploys that automatically to a staging environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You've got your staging review process.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then at some point you deploy to master, you know, all of that, just have it automated with the exception of like the peer review process.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that's probably a big one for most people.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's why CI CD gets so much scope of the DevOps conversation, even though I think it's such a small piece of the overall DevOps framework.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then the other one is probably the ops part of DevOps.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Logging, alerting and monitoring.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: logging okay is there a certain tools uh that folks should look into for for logging or should they be logging to a particular like part of linux should they be just using the logging framework that's in their system and how do they monitor those logs or what do you recommend there if you were to walk into a startup and they don't have this stuff running what do you how would you implement it if they were using aws i would use cloud watch logs okay um
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: especially if you're doing something like AWS's ECS, their elastic container service.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It makes it really easy because it just captures the standard out from your Docker containers and will ship that over to CloudWatch Logs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If they don't have that, the ELK stack, Elasticsearch Logstash Kibana is a great way to go.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then if you've just got tons of cash to spend and don't mind blowing it, there's tools like Splunk that are available.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, I've heard a lot of big companies use Splunk, especially big companies after they have a lot more budget.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It seems to be very expensive.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, it's incredibly expensive.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And what do they call it?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Oh, it's a sticky product because once you start using it, you're like,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'll just write the check this month rather than try to figure out how to decouple all of this.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's what I heard, yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So you said logging, monitoring, what was the last one there?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Alerting.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Alerting, okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So I've got maybe logging set up and then if I'm going to monitor these logs, what should I be looking for in these logs?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So the monitoring part actually doesn't go with the logs a whole lot.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: When I talk about monitoring, I typically think of like API response times and the rate of error codes.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So HTTP 200s means everything's good.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Those are always great to have, but what's the response time on those?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: My personal preference is to keep your API responses under 200 milliseconds.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So keep an eye on that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What is our response time?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Also payload size.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then for the errors, you know, when you have HTTP, something in the 400 series or the 500 series, not only are those happening and the count of those, but the rate, because a lot of times the rate is your early indicator.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If your error rate for HTTP 500s was at 0.001%.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then it jumps to 0.1%.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's a pretty significant change.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And somebody should probably be taking a look at what's going on there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So for monitoring, what types of tools should someone look at for that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Or where can they kind of start depending upon their stack?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: AWS, again, goes into the CloudWatch framework.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: CloudWatch covers your logging, monitoring, and alerting.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Mm-hmm.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: with the kibana using the elk stack kibana will allow you to graph that out and then there's there's just a whole host of tools you can use beyond that i think one of the most common ones now that's gaining a lot of popularity is prometheus with grafana
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Now, if I'm on Heroku, is there tools on there to help monitor these types of things?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Or am I kind of out of luck?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: No, no, not by any means.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's another one of those places where Heroku has done a great job of doing a lot of integration and bundling services to make it just a button click for you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's the built-in Heroku logging.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: which will show you some level of logging and then when you log in there's the monitoring dashboard right there showing you a lot of these metrics we were just talking about and then you can dig in and add third-party add-ons where they can integrate you with um with um well i've seen like i've seen elastic and i've seen like i think i've seen elk stack stuff out there and like paper trail and stuff like that where you can do like logging and all kinds of probably other monitoring stuff too
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, PaperTrail is one of their big logging solutions.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Scout was the one I was trying to remember.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Oh, Scout.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, Scout APM is real good.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, and PagerDuty as well for doing the alerting portion of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah, Scout actually saved my butt when I was working on Caster a while back, and I could not figure out why I was always getting R14 errors.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And those of you that are familiar with Heroku, R14 means your server's out of memory.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And I would just jack the memory on my dynos, and it would still, it would just run out of memory.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I had a memory leak somewhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: and it wasn't until I installed Scout.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So this is not a sponsored thing, but it's just I installed it, and they literally showed me, here's where your problem is, here's where your problem is, here's where your problem is, and I fixed about 70% of the problems, and the majority of my problems with Heroku stuff went away.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So definitely highly worth looking into.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Was it APM application performance monitoring?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is that what it is?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yep, exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: New Relic's another big player in that field.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That is, yes.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And then you had alerting.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is that kind of in the same context of monitoring or what entails alerting?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, so your alerting is triggered by your monitoring.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So like we were talking a minute ago about the rate of HTTP 500 errors, if that exceeds a certain threshold, you know you'll want to trigger an alert for that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: For your success responses, your 200 responses, maybe you want to monitor the latency, and if latency exceeds 500 milliseconds, you want an alert for that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then on the other side of that too, you may want...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: like a low side alert too.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if your HTTP 200 requests drop down to zero, it probably shouldn't be happening.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So you want an alert to see, did something go down or did all of your customers just stop using your product?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Don't want that.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Hopefully the former, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Right.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You mentioned something earlier about the payload size on HTTP.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And we both worked at a client once that had, when we came in, their payload size of the JSON response.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I think it was 40, 10,000 or 40,000 lines of JSON.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: It was something just astronomic.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: How does that impact like response times or anything like that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Or does it at all?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Oh, it does.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It does.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You've got the, first of all, to generate 40,000 lines of JSON, you've got some compute time tied up in there somewhere.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then you've got the network latency that's added to transmitting that data back to it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's probably the bigger impact of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Because when you look at your server API response time, that's the response time for the data to leave the server.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's still not accounting for the network transit time for it to get back to your client wherever they may happen to be.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That's true.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Especially if you're transferring that much data, it's just, that's a lot.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Even just for time, the first byte, that's a lot.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, and I think that's one of the key areas where mobile developers can take an active role in the backend and the DevOps process.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Because DevOps really is not a single person.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's something that we all have a different stake in.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And specifically for the mobile developer to take a look at the API endpoints that they're calling...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and see what the response time looks like from the client perspective and the payload itself.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Make sure that, you know, that the payload's reasonably sized and that it just contains the data that you're looking for.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That was the problem we ran into with that one client is that was like a God mode API where it returned, you know, just everything that you could possibly imagine, even though they only wanted one key out of that payload.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: You basically had to cache the whole thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And once you refreshed it, it took a few seconds for it to go get all that data.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah.

text: So you bring up a good point there is that this is kind of everybody's job, which, you know, I think is a big question for everyone listening is, okay, this all sounds cool.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: I don't have any clue how to do that automation with the, you know, the branches to develop, uh, when I push a branch up and it's in a pull request to have it build another platform, you know,
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Is that something I can go learn?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Where do I learn that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Or do I need to go hire a DevOps person?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Or when do I hire a DevOps person?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So it's multiple questions there.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: What's your thoughts on that?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: Yeah, I think you rely on the resources that you have in your team for smaller teams.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Smaller teams with senior engineers between those members, they're probably each going to have enough info to pull together the pieces that you need.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You might not know how to do a CI CD workflow, but someone else on the team might.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so you let them handle that and you take care of the parts that you are competent with.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think that's why I push back so much on hiring.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: a DevOps engineer, you know, or giving someone that title because then it pins that responsibility on them and resolves everyone else.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I like to think of it more.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I have a buddy who was a Navy SEAL for 20 years and in the SEAL teams, everyone there has like a couple of different roles in the team based on their expertise.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Like he was a sniper and the medic with whatever level of irony goes with that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: yeah but um but that's the way it was you know he had those two roles and someone else was like a you know a heavy gunner and the explosions expert but then you put all of these different people together with these different skill sets and you've got like this full on force capable of addressing the problem
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I think engineering teams are the same way.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You have this engineering team where someone has CICD workflow experience and someone else has monitoring and logging experience.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And so then whenever the team gets together and starts to architect a new product or an update to an existing product, each person interjects with their particular expertise to build out the whole product.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: eventually you'll hit a point where your team grows enough where you've got engineers of varying levels so you've got senior engineers and junior engineers and i think at that point that might be the the right time to give someone who's got a more devops oriented role to
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: to go and have that social interaction with different members of the team as they're building out these products to make sure that they're following the devops principles that the teams agreed upon and that may or may not be a full-time
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: role for that person you know for me a lot of times i've been considered the devops person but a lot of times i was just writing application code and then sitting in on meetings and having conversations with people you know to help them build tools according to the pipelines that we've put together
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So it seems like you kind of just have to keep track of how much DevOps time you're putting in on something across the team.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And eventually, like you said, you might be 50% DevOps, 50% developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: And again, companies allocate, I hate to use the word resources for people, but that's what they call it now, allocate resources differently.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: But they need to know, all right, we're now using Will 75% of his time, he's just spending on DevOps.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: So
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: If we need to give dev work to him, we need to make sure we give dev work to him that's not critical.
speaker: SPEAKER_02

text: We'll give that to Jane over here where she's full-time front-end or back-end engineering with minimal DevOps stuff and just kind of know how to handle and kind of crack that nut as you move forward, right?
speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: then the DevOps person will go and make sure that there's a Redis instance provisioned and configured by the time they're ready to deploy.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02

speaker: SPEAKER_02
